Case Study: Human RNA-seq Workflow

Source: vignettes/case-study-human-rnaseq.Rmd
case-study-human-rnaseq.Rmd

Goal

This case study shows a simple human RNA-seq workflow in expranno, starting from expr and meta, then moving through annotation, integration, and output generation.

Step 1: Input tables 

demo$expr
#>              gene_id sample_a sample_b sample_c
#> 1 ENSG00000141510.17      120      140      118
#> 2 ENSG00000146648.18       80       77       91
#> 3 ENSG00000012048.23       25       30       21
demo$meta
#>     sample   group batch species
#> 1 sample_a    case    b1   human
#> 2 sample_b control    b1   human
#> 3 sample_c    case    b2   human

Step 2: Validate the input contract 

validate_expr(demo$expr)
validate_meta(demo$meta)

Step 3: Choose an annotation engine

For human data, the recommended production choice is:

  • annotation_engine = "hybrid"

That setting tries:

  1. biomaRt
  2. org.Hs.eg.db
  3. optional EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86

For the website build, we use "none" so the article stays lightweight. To still show what a successful annotation pass looks like, the article also includes a small mock output table and coverage report.

anno <- annotate_expr(
  expr = demo$expr,
  meta = demo$meta,
  species = "human",
  annotation_engine = "none"
)

anno$expr_anno
#>          gene_id_raw         gene_id symbol gene_name entrez_id biotype
#> 1 ENSG00000141510.17 ENSG00000141510     NA        NA        NA      NA
#> 2 ENSG00000146648.18 ENSG00000146648     NA        NA        NA      NA
#> 3 ENSG00000012048.23 ENSG00000012048     NA        NA        NA      NA
#>   chromosome start end strand annotation_source annotation_status sample_a
#> 1         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated      120
#> 2         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated       80
#> 3         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated       25
#>   sample_b sample_c
#> 1      140      118
#> 2       77       91
#> 3       30       21

Step 4: Inspect expr_anno.csv

In a real run, this table is what gets written to expr_anno.csv. Below is a compact mock excerpt that shows the kind of annotated output you should expect from a successful human run with "hybrid".

mock_expr_anno
#>          gene_id_raw         gene_id symbol
#> 1 ENSG00000141510.17 ENSG00000141510   TP53
#> 2 ENSG00000146648.18 ENSG00000146648   EGFR
#> 3 ENSG00000157764.15 ENSG00000157764   BRAF
#>                                       gene_name annotation_status
#> 1                             tumor protein p53         annotated
#> 2              epidermal growth factor receptor         annotated
#> 3 B-Raf proto-oncogene, serine/threonine kinase         annotated

The main columns to check are:

  • gene_id_raw
  • gene_id
  • symbol
  • gene_name
  • annotation_status

Step 5: Inspect the annotation report

expranno returns a compact report table that summarizes how many genes received each annotation field.

mock_report
#>       field annotation_rate
#> 1    symbol            1.00
#> 2 gene_name            1.00
#> 3 entrez_id            0.67
#> 4   biotype            1.00

Step 6: How to read annotation coverage

The annotation_rate column is the proportion of genes with a non-missing value for each field in the annotated gene table.

The main practical checks are:

  1. symbol coverage should be high enough for downstream symbol-based tools
  2. gene_name coverage should usually track with symbol coverage
  3. entrez_id can be a little lower, but large gaps still deserve attention
  4. low rates suggest rerunning with "hybrid" and checking installed backends

In this mock example, symbol and gene_name are fully covered, while entrez_id coverage is partial. That pattern is often acceptable if the next steps mainly depend on symbols, but it would still be worth noting in the analysis record.

Step 7: Merge annotated expression with metadata 

merged <- merge_expr_meta(
  expr_anno = anno$expr_anno,
  meta = anno$meta_checked
)

merged
#>     sample        gene_id_raw         gene_id symbol gene_name entrez_id
#> 1 sample_a ENSG00000141510.17 ENSG00000141510     NA        NA        NA
#> 2 sample_a ENSG00000146648.18 ENSG00000146648     NA        NA        NA
#> 3 sample_a ENSG00000012048.23 ENSG00000012048     NA        NA        NA
#> 4 sample_b ENSG00000141510.17 ENSG00000141510     NA        NA        NA
#> 5 sample_b ENSG00000146648.18 ENSG00000146648     NA        NA        NA
#> 6 sample_b ENSG00000012048.23 ENSG00000012048     NA        NA        NA
#> 7 sample_c ENSG00000141510.17 ENSG00000141510     NA        NA        NA
#> 8 sample_c ENSG00000146648.18 ENSG00000146648     NA        NA        NA
#> 9 sample_c ENSG00000012048.23 ENSG00000012048     NA        NA        NA
#>   biotype chromosome start end strand annotation_source annotation_status
#> 1      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 2      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 3      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 4      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 5      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 6      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 7      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 8      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#> 9      NA         NA    NA  NA     NA              <NA>       unannotated
#>   expression   group batch species
#> 1        120    case    b1   human
#> 2         80    case    b1   human
#> 3         25    case    b1   human
#> 4        140 control    b1   human
#> 5         77 control    b1   human
#> 6         30 control    b1   human
#> 7        118    case    b2   human
#> 8         91    case    b2   human
#> 9         21    case    b2   human

Step 8: Inspect a simple sample summary 

aggregate(expression ~ sample + group, data = merged, FUN = mean)
#>     sample   group expression
#> 1 sample_a    case   75.00000
#> 2 sample_c    case   76.66667
#> 3 sample_b control   82.33333

Step 9: Run the standard wrapper 

out <- run_expranno(
  expr = demo$expr,
  meta = demo$meta,
  species = "human",
  annotation_engine = "none",
  output_dir = tempdir(),
  run_deconvolution = FALSE,
  run_signature = FALSE
)

out
#> <expranno_result>
#>   annotated genes: 3
#>   merged rows: 9
#>   deconvolution runs: 0
#>   signature runs: 0

Human workflow takeaway

For a real human analysis, the usual sequence is:

  1. annotate with "hybrid"
  2. inspect expr_anno.csv
  3. inspect the annotation coverage report
  4. merge with meta
  5. run deconvolution
  6. run GSVA or ssGSEA

On this page

Developed by Daiki User.
